That's why I'm stepping in today.
Yeah.
Okay, great.
Great.
Um, that's awesome.
I am.
Okay, I think I actually just thought heat leave to it.
I was like
Oh, I'm going to ping him.
We shouldn't get started until June 9th.
Okay.
How have things been going, though, Jessica?
I know that we didn't connect during, like, the sales process, but how have things been?
Yeah.
So far, very good.
Awesome.
Great.
So he was just getting a conference booth behind me.
Let's see.
Yeah, I feel like the tone feels – I feel like we struck the right tone.
So I was really happy to see that.
Hex is pretty guarded about, like, how we come across.
So it feels like we nailed that, and that was great.
And then also the articles feel really good.
Hey, Pete, they feel like the right level of depth.

I believe she gave the thumbs up, the green light.
Like, um… ...

And then I don't know, Pete, if we want to talk about some of the stuff that came up yesterday in terms of like ranking for AI, but I'll leave it at that.
Pete, were just, Tyler just asked me how things were going so far and just getting an overall check in.
So cool.
appreciate that.
I call the tail end of Jess's overview.
And from what I heard that, I agree.
I think this, I guess, not, I guess, second edition, third total iteration of the content piece, I think was much, much better.
I was, in particular, you know, you know, voice and tone, Jess, I'll let her speak to all of that.
In particular, I was reassured to see that like comparison table.
I think that positioning piece is a tricky one that we've struggled for a long time and talking about competitors and distinguishing approach.
It is subtle.
And I was impressed by how that turned out in this, you know, in only one iteration and sort of ran that by our channels or had a product marketing and, you know, that was reassuring.
And in terms of the rest of the content, like Jess said, we had a new, like, more technical, more data-oriented evangelist review.
And she checked.
I mean, she also understood right away.
She's like, this is for SEO.
It's like not the sexiest article in the world, but it's like, it's like on the nose.
It's accurate.
Like, it would be helpful to someone trying to learn about this.

I don't know, Jess, if you've had a chance to review the Jupiter versus Jupiter Lab and then the, I think, I can't remember.
No, I just started to get into those.
Sorry.
I can do them right after the call today.
Um, so, but I just took a quick skim.
The length looks really nice.
It's another thing I was going to comment on.
Sometimes we've gotten SEO articles back that were just so, so, so long.
Um, I don't know.
I mean, maybe it works, but I appreciate that.
Like, uh, yeah, we covered the topic in a digestible way.
And then if there's something that people need to leap into that, then they can, I think more and more just valuing the reader experience and making that, um, useful and enjoyable for them.
Um, I still prioritize that a little bit more.
Um, if we got great data that super long articles did well, then like, yeah, we can lean into that.
So I will take a closer look right after we hop off this call, but yeah, apologies.
We've been on off sites for the last two days.
Yeah, no, no problem.
And I'll let Aida kind of run through the agenda, but, um, I've kind of been watching from afar, just seeing how things are going.
Right.
Cause there's obviously like,
A lot of clients that we're working with in parallel, but we'd be conscious of not kicking off with too many at one time.

But one pattern that we've noticed that was maybe coming up a little bit here is we want to make sure that we can get through calibration quickly so that we can start cranking out articles since scale is so important to success.
And we obviously want to scale with quality, but also the artifacts are living documents.
So we'll continue to make updates to those.

And I think we're in a good, I can see we're all aligned here now, like on let's start reviewing some of these article drafts that we've created so that we can publish them more quickly and get some early signals for you.

But let's also think from like the SEO GEO lens, how can we streamline the review process so that we're not overanalyzing like every single word and maybe having like four different stakeholders review when we could just narrow it down to one or two, if we can not have a bottleneck.
In the review process, then we can get to results a lot faster.

And I just want to make sure that we can publish some articles before we get to that last week where the opt-out from phase two is, so that you can at least see some early signals of what's working, what's not, and have confidence with whichever direction you decide to go.
So definitely feel free to respond or share some thoughts on everything I just put out there, or we can jump into Aida's agenda for the call today.
I mean, that sounds like the right concern.
I guess my question for you is, if there's some SLA that we've been missing, let us know, first of all.
And second of all, what is the right, in successful partnerships you guys have had, what is that typical turnaround time for you to deliver a draft to review?
Let's call it in the first few months, I know what's called the first three months, probably different than the preceding nine months.
I would assume it gets easier and easier and easier to review.

But, like, 24 hours, 48 hours, like, what are we, tell us, based on your experience, how we can, yeah, so I think, so the cadence that we want to get into is, okay, we deliver five by the end of the week, and we publish five by the end of the week.

So it's kind of, like, if we deliver, like, five articles on Friday, getting your feedback, I guess, like, on Thursday, like, at the latest would be helpful, so that we can, like, go and implement the feedback, and then we can stage the content on Friday, and you can publish it, and then you get another five.

So it doesn't have to be, like, super fast, like, 24, 48 hours, it can be, like, like, three or four days, but that is, like, just to get into that rhythm of, like, you get five, we publish five, and that's kind of, like, a good rhythm to have with the production.
We do quite fast.
We can do revisions quite fast.
So unless it's a complete rewrite and we have to do everything from scratch, we can implement feedback pretty quickly.
Cool.
I think we can agree to that.
I don't know that we can cut it to two reviewers for now.
Jess, my immediate thoughts are, I think you need, once you feel okay with tone, you can honestly just give these a glance over and maybe you can follow up.
I think I want Rachel, that's our more technical resource we were talking about.
I think I want her to stay reviewing these for a little bit longer.
And then I'll review them as well as a blend of like, if anything, sniff test on positioning.
Seems like I want to get a gut check just so I can go flag that with the powers that be.
I think we can get that down to three.
Hopefully, Jess, it becomes pretty light for you.
Yeah.
I would hope the tone is especially as something that like will get better and better and better very quickly.
The positioning, I think, just might take a little more, you know, a little more time.
Yeah.
Yeah.
And just to clarify, like, no worries about how the process is currently going.
Just wanted to make sure in your best interest that we're able to publish at a good volume as the partnership continues.

And also that, like, you have enough signals and samples from us so that, you know, on December 15th, right, as you can see, like, the opt-out date for Phase 1 to Phase 2, you, like, feel good either way about the direction you're taking.
You know, you're confident about that.
Yeah.
But by no means do you have to, like, cut it to two reviewers for this to work.
Not saying that.
Cool.

No, aligned, and I appreciate if we are lagging or something, I'd rather proactively call us out on that so that we can keep this moving because I similarly want to, yeah, get as much signal as we can and scale here.
Cool.
So we're aligned.
All right.
Perfect.
So, yeah.
So, for the content updates this week, so these are the four that are ready for review.
One is from this week's batch, and you're going to get four more tomorrow.
So, that's five for this week.
And we did stage the calibration in your CMS.
We just had one question about who do we want to mention in the author field.
Do we want to maybe see if Rachel's comfortable having her name on these, Jess?
Yeah, we could ask her.
Or it could be, I don't know.
Is it better to have someone with, like, Aida in their title, or?
I think, I mean, ideally, yes.
But a real person is always better than just, like, Hex.
So, even if it's someone, you know, who isn't, like, a data expert, it's fine.
As long as it's, like, a real person.
a person.
information, visit
Let's ask Rachel and see what she thinks.
I'll message her.
She's reviewing them too, so.
Okay, yeah, so it's not like we would be asking her to, like, be the author of something she's never seen.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Good.
All right.
Yeah.
Just let us know either way.
But yeah, that's, that one's pretty much ready to go.
You can take a look and let us know if we, like, filled out everything correctly.
Um, but yeah, as soon as you, you know, under, like, know who the author is, can go ahead and, um, publish it.
We did use, um, one of the featured image examples that I sent last week.
Um, and I was wondering if you got any feedback on, just, like, from the team on, on the design.
Candidly, I didn't ask.
To be honest, I think these actually look, like, very in line with the types of imagery that our team produces.
Um, I think we're fine to go with an ask forgiveness approach on these, on this first article I can check with.
If our team, if there's any like notable qualms, but.
Okay.
Yeah, I think.
They seem low stakes.
The only thing, Pete, I mean, it's getting kind of technical.
The colors are associated pretty clearly with like specific solutions.
So maybe moving forward, I wonder if we could just ask Annie if there's another brand color that we could use.
I mean, I'm sure these all map to a specific solution anyway.
So yeah, let's ask for forgiveness and then, Pete, maybe we can take that back.
Cool.
Yeah, mean, these also like wouldn't be showcased side by side with our solutions content.
Yeah, yeah, that's fair.
That's fair.
Yes, we need to go check with her.
But overall, I think these are actually like pretty in line with a lot of our brand imagery.
Okay, cool.
All right.
So I think which one do you.

I this version, or hold on, what was the other one, or this one, because there are two different pipelines, so this one is kind of like more, I think almost backgrounds are very similar, but the other one is a bit more graphic, this one I think uses images for like the center image, and then the other one uses illustrations, so I don't know, you can let me know, if you like both, I'll tell Katya that you like both, and she can figure it out, but yeah, there are two different versions in here, so we just need to let her know which ones she should integrate with the main article generation pipeline, because each image is going to like be generated with the article itself.
I don't know if I have a huge preference.
I was going to say, I think they're both fine.
Some of these images are a little dark.
Like there's like a dark on dark for some of these that.
Yeah.
Like that, like that laptop at the bottom.
Right.
Yeah.
But like, although most of the left-hand column, I think is good.
Like the calculator, a little swirly background, the charts on the right.
Like, I think that's pretty brand aligned for us.
Is there an easy way to like access this, these folders to like, just share, you know, a bigger sample size with our, our brand lead for review?
I can upload them.
I can ask Katya to upload them to a Figma file and then you can share it.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Because this is an atlas and atlas is just growthx.
So we can just export them.
Yeah.
If you can export them, like I said, I think we're fine on this first article.
Okay.
Okay.
That makes sense.
But yeah, we'll have them.
And hopefully you can sort of train the future output based on that.
Okay, cool.
could I actually jump in real quick and just call out in the, this is so helpful for the upload and contentful.
The only thing I'm wondering is if we can just stick in an author initials.
And for Rachel, actually, there we go.
It would be like RH.
RH, basically, if there's, if this is read or the date is read, it actually prevents us from publishing anything else in Contentful, which is kind of crazy.
But if, if a draft has an empty blank open and somebody else, now we have a few more publishers and then like trying to find where the empty draft states are can be a little bit of a crazy process.
So if, if y'all just want to fill in a date, like we're happy to change it, but that's just my only request.
It's not a big deal.
We can, we can figure it out, but I just try to prevent that where possible.
I think the rest of us is fine.
got it.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Okay, cool.
That's good.
All right.
Cool.
And then for, I don't think I have any other, excuse me.
I don't think I have any other content updates for today.
Do you have anything else that you want to discuss?
Otherwise, I can show you Atlas and how it works.
Yeah, I'd love to see that.
Another thing is if we have like a stack ranked, like here's the roadmap and here's the order.
I don't remember going through that together other than like the big list that we get feedback on.
But just like how we're ordering these, I'd be curious to take a look at and see if there's any adjustments needed.
Or if there's a rhyme or reason to like understand why you guys.
Yeah, yeah.
I think it would be helpful if I just prepare that in a Notion document and then you can kind of go through and leave feedback.
Yeah.
Just to help you understand why, like, what we're prioritizing and how.
Cool.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Okay.
All right.
So for Atlas, so to give you a little bit of a background, so we launched Atlas, I believe, in May or April this year, and it was built by our developers.
And prior to this, were using AirOps.
And since launch, it's evolved a lot.
So these are all, like, the different pipelines that we have.

We experiment a lot with the pipelines because we're constantly trying to figure out, okay, how can we get the best possible output in a shorter amount of time and, like, reduce review cycles for everyone?

Which is why, like, Kavishka, who's, like, the editor who's creating your content, he has, like, a test pipeline set up where he can kind of experiment with the artifacts and, like, without impacting the actual articles that we're creating for you.
So, like,
And we also have, you can see that here we have like non-agentic article generation and agentic article generation.
And the non-agentic article generation pipeline was an old one that we actually never used with HEX.
So when you came on as a client, we started rolling out agentic pipelines for all of our clients.
So the reason it's called an agentic pipeline, because it grades itself at specific steps, in addition to the human review.
So the article gen pipeline, it's kind of, we broke up the article generation process into different chunks.
And it starts by, with a title.
So we provide it with a topic.
Aida, are you sharing your screen?
Oh my God.
Aida, you sharing your Sorry.
No, that's okay.
Yeah.
So this is...
I stopped sharing my screen.
Right.
So this is your workspace.
So you can kind of see like all the different pipelines in here.
This is where your artifacts live.
And then to go back, this is the agentic article generation pipeline that we have set up for you.
So it starts with, we start with a topic, which is basically the article title.
And then we, in the input, we provide it different, like we provide it with different information to guide the outline and the research process.
So we don't just give it the title, but we give it a whole assignment direction.
And some of these variable parameters, they're identified by AI.
So we have the assignment persona, we have the audience personas in the context artifacts.
So while it's...
Generating the outline, it's going to pick like the primary persona that's most relevant for a piece of content.

And then we have like some general content requirements that is applied across all articles, just to make sure that the information that's being used is new, that it's using high quality sources, linking to like verified sources.
But again, these are all things that we can change.

So if we notice that the output isn't working, like it's not linking to the right sources, or it's just kind of missing some information, we can like change, completely change this assignment direction template and use something different.
So it gives us a lot of like freedom to experiment.
And then we come to the assignment direction, which is something that is human generated in the sense that we use our week.

Do our own research on a specific keyword to understand what does the SERP look like, what's the top-ranking content, what is the article skeleton that we need to have in order for a good quality article.
And we do a lot of manual research, and we then combine all of that information, and we generate an outline that we provide here.
We kind of, like, you know, give it, like, quick bullet points for each H2 and H3.
And then once we're happy with this, we move on to the research step, and this is a very important step.
We use Tavali for, as a researcher, we used to use perplexity, but perplexity was just, we find that Tavali is a lot, it's more powerful in terms of, like, finding high-quality information.
So what the researcher does is it looks at the brief.
And then it asks questions that it has to answer in order to generate the draft.
So it answers these questions and it generates a very comprehensive research document that it pulls from while it's generating the draft.

I don't know if you ever tried to generate an article and just chat CPT and told it, okay, write an article about this topic, and you give it like five bullet points, you'll notice that the article is like just repeating the same things over and over again.
And that's because it doesn't have the research.
So with this step, we make sure that the AI has enough information to write a good quality article.
And then after the researcher is done running, it drafts the article.
And this is when we typically will kind of scan the article and make sure that it followed the outline that we provided.
In the assignment direction, just to make sure that nothing changed, or if anything changed, that we kind of verified that we wanted those changes to happen, and they're good additions.
And we run through the additional steps of like adding internal links, adding source links to any stats or industry reports that were mentioned.
And then this step, validate writing guidelines, is really important because we apply the writing guidelines at the draft stage.
But what happens sometimes is that LLMs might follow some instructions from the writing guidelines, mean, Claude, and then ignore others.
Or sometimes the writing instructions, without us realizing it, might include instructions that are kind of opposing each other, and some of them might not be applied at the draft stage.
So we added an additional step where the AI will grade the draft against the writing guidelines, and then score it on a scale of 1 to 0.
Here, the confidence score is 0.85, and it gives us an overview of the changes that it implemented to the draft to make sure that it followed the writing guidelines better.
So, for example, it applied fixes like removing competitor mentions, replaced generic references, fixed hyperlinks, added bullet points.
So the changes are always different depending on the initial draft.
But it basically allows us to kind of control the output better.
Then we run the fact checker, and I realize we're at time.
So the fact checker basically divides the article into chunks of information that has to be verified.
And then it will manually, the researcher will manually then check each chunk and ensure...
If it's not correct, then it's going to remove it from the draft, or if it can't find the source, then it's going to tell us that it can't find the source.
But it's going to edit the draft basically in real time as it's finding this information.
I don't know if you have, like, just a minute or two.
I can show you actually, like, in the back end.
Yeah.
Right.
So this is Flow Studio.
This allows us to kind of see, like, the back end process of Atlas.
So this is the content fact checker workflow.
So you can see, like, okay, the first step is to split article into chunks.
And then it extracts passages.
So, for example, this is the passage that it identified.
The AI generates SQL automatically, so you focus on questions instead of syntax, blah, blah, blah.
And then it gives a reason why it extracted this passage for, like, fact checking.
And then it poses different questions that it has to answer in order to fact check.

This piece of information and it does that for each passage it identifies and then it researches the passage and then it basically like combines this information to fact check each passage and then it goes back and edits them depending on the different findings.
Wow, this is wild.
It gives me a lot more confidence in these articles.

Yeah, and Aida, if you go down further, which maybe you're planning to do, yeah, the verify passage piece is really cool because this is where it validates if it's accurate or if there's some nuance, right, where we can't guarantee and if there's some nuance, it'll actually suggest a rewrite to solve for that nuance.
Yeah.

So sometimes when we're reading a draft and, you know, we read like, although there is no specific data to back up a specific point, the evidence does support that a specific outcome might be created by some platform.
And then we know, oh, this is the fact checker, like the fact checker added this information in so we can decide, do we want to keep this?
Do we want to do manual research to replace the, you know, the stat with something that's like, you know, more specific.
But yeah, the fact checker has evolved a lot since I joined growthx and we continue improving it as like we have, you know, we have clients really all over, like all different industries.
And each industry has its own, you know, just, like just kind of demands in terms of fact checking.
So it is something that we're continually improving.
And yeah, final.

Point is that this agentic pipeline, we can modify it in the sense that we can add additional steps here, depending on like, if we need to do like a product fact check, if it turns out that we need to like, or add like additional product mentions in a way that's automated, and it saves us a lot of time, we can also add a step here that kind of identifies, okay, what are like the top, what are like the best places to insert a hex mention in a way that's organic, and the AI will do that using like a separate artifact.
And yeah, any other questions I can help answer?
No, super cool.
There are times where when we're reading drafts, I mean, I've, like Jess said, that's a lot of peace of mind baked into that thorough process.
There are some times where we're curious to see what the source is.
Does that have to be like tagging you in the doc?
There's no easier way for us to go figure out like where certain claims and articles are coming from?
Yeah, I think if you are are
We're really curious about where a piece of information came from.
can just tag us right now because Atlas is just accessible to us.
So you can just let us know what you're interested in and we'll find it.
Cool.
Aida, are there certain publications, I guess I just asked this because another agency we've worked with, sometimes they're pulling from like one-off publications I've not heard of.
And so we actually gave them like a list of five that we, it's like McKinsey, Gartner, I don't know, just names people would know, Forbes, maybe.
Does that system use maybe a list of those like first primary sources or does it just pull?
Yeah, we can provide primary sources.
Like we can tell it, like we have, we have, for example, one client that's in a very kind of regulated industry and they only want to link to .org and like ResearchGate and we...
Basically make sure that nothing else is like being cited so we can control that in the assignment direction.
Okay, cool.
I'll surface that list of what we gave them and then, yeah, I'm kind of curious to see what it comes up with on its own, but just to have that in the background when we need it.
Cool.
All right.
Well, yeah, if you don't have any questions, we can wrap up and yeah, we'll be in touch with the five articles for this week soon.
Awesome.
Thanks, Aida.
And Peter and Jessica, if, you know, you need to set up a conversation between me and Brianna or anybody else, Ashley, ahead of the 15th, just let me know.
Happy to like, talk through, you know, the decision on phase two.
Awesome.
Thank you.
See you next week.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Align on content review process and production cadence.](https://fathom.video/share/nadTYn2QH2smPEMFxjzDh2RpgXdA8EYD?tab=summary&timestamp=366.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [**New Cadence:** Adopt a 5-article/week cycle (drafts delivered Fri, feedback by Thu) to generate performance signals before the Dec 15 Phase 2 opt-out date.](https://fathom.video/share/nadTYn2QH2smPEMFxjzDh2RpgXdA8EYD?tab=summary&timestamp=480.0)
  - [**Streamlined Review:** Reduce Hex's review team to three stakeholders (Jessica, Peter, Rachel) to accelerate feedback and unblock the new cadence.](https://fathom.video/share/nadTYn2QH2smPEMFxjzDh2RpgXdA8EYD?tab=summary&timestamp=555.0)
  - [**Content Quality:** The agentic Atlas platform uses AI to self-grade against guidelines and fact-check every passage, ensuring high accuracy and brand consistency.](https://fathom.video/share/nadTYn2QH2smPEMFxjzDh2RpgXdA8EYD?tab=summary&timestamp=1460.0)
  - [**Immediate Actions:** Jessica will confirm Rachel as the author; Peter will get brand approval for featured images; Aida will share the content roadmap.](https://fathom.video/share/nadTYn2QH2smPEMFxjzDh2RpgXdA8EYD?tab=summary&timestamp=682.0)

## Topics

### Content Review & Cadence

  - [**Problem:** The current review process is a bottleneck, delaying publication and preventing the generation of performance signals needed for the Dec 15 Phase 2 decision.](https://fathom.video/share/nadTYn2QH2smPEMFxjzDh2RpgXdA8EYD?tab=summary&timestamp=366.0)
  - [**Solution:** Adopt a 5-article/week production cadence.](https://fathom.video/share/nadTYn2QH2smPEMFxjzDh2RpgXdA8EYD?tab=summary&timestamp=480.0)
      - [**GrowthX:** Delivers 5 drafts on Friday.](https://fathom.video/share/nadTYn2QH2smPEMFxjzDh2RpgXdA8EYD?tab=summary&timestamp=480.0)
      - [**Hex:** Provides feedback by the following Thursday.](https://fathom.video/share/nadTYn2QH2smPEMFxjzDh2RpgXdA8EYD?tab=summary&timestamp=480.0)
      - [**GrowthX:** Implements revisions and stages content for publication on Friday.](https://fathom.video/share/nadTYn2QH2smPEMFxjzDh2RpgXdA8EYD?tab=summary&timestamp=480.0)
  - [**Review Team:** Reduced to three stakeholders to accelerate feedback.](https://fathom.video/share/nadTYn2QH2smPEMFxjzDh2RpgXdA8EYD?tab=summary&timestamp=555.0)
      - [**Jessica:** Tone (expected to become a light review).](https://fathom.video/share/nadTYn2QH2smPEMFxjzDh2RpgXdA8EYD?tab=summary&timestamp=555.0)
      - [**Rachel:** Technical accuracy.](https://fathom.video/share/nadTYn2QH2smPEMFxjzDh2RpgXdA8EYD?tab=summary&timestamp=555.0)
      - [**Peter:** Positioning and gut check.](https://fathom.video/share/nadTYn2QH2smPEMFxjzDh2RpgXdA8EYD?tab=summary&timestamp=555.0)

### Atlas Platform Deep Dive

  - [Aida demonstrated Atlas, GrowthX's proprietary platform, to build confidence in the content's quality and accuracy.](https://fathom.video/share/nadTYn2QH2smPEMFxjzDh2RpgXdA8EYD?tab=summary&timestamp=1041.0)
  - [**Key Features:**](https://fathom.video/share/nadTYn2QH2smPEMFxjzDh2RpgXdA8EYD?tab=summary&timestamp=1140.0)
      - [**Agentic Pipeline:** AI self-grades against guidelines and fact-checks every passage, ensuring consistency and accuracy.](https://fathom.video/share/nadTYn2QH2smPEMFxjzDh2RpgXdA8EYD?tab=summary&timestamp=1460.0)
      - [**Research:** Uses Tavali (replacing Perplexity) for more powerful, high-quality information gathering.](https://fathom.video/share/nadTYn2QH2smPEMFxjzDh2RpgXdA8EYD?tab=summary&timestamp=1355.0)
      - [**Fact-Checking:** Divides articles into passages, researches each claim, and edits the draft in real-time.](https://fathom.video/share/nadTYn2QH2smPEMFxjzDh2RpgXdA8EYD?tab=summary&timestamp=1536.0)
      - [**Customization:** The pipeline is modular; new steps can be added for product-specific checks or organic product mentions.](https://fathom.video/share/nadTYn2QH2smPEMFxjzDh2RpgXdA8EYD?tab=summary&timestamp=1738.0)

### Logistics & Content Details

  - [**Author:** Jessica will confirm if Rachel is comfortable being listed as the author.](https://fathom.video/share/nadTYn2QH2smPEMFxjzDh2RpgXdA8EYD?tab=summary&timestamp=682.0)
      - [**Rationale:** A real person's name is preferred over a generic company name.](https://fathom.video/share/nadTYn2QH2smPEMFxjzDh2RpgXdA8EYD?tab=summary&timestamp=695.0)
  - [**Featured Images:**](https://fathom.video/share/nadTYn2QH2smPEMFxjzDh2RpgXdA8EYD?tab=summary&timestamp=752.0)
      - [**Issue:** The first article's image uses a color associated with a specific Hex solution.](https://fathom.video/share/nadTYn2QH2smPEMFxjzDh2RpgXdA8EYD?tab=summary&timestamp=790.0)
      - [**Decision:** Publish the first article as-is. Peter will get brand approval from Annie for future images.](https://fathom.video/share/nadTYn2QH2smPEMFxjzDh2RpgXdA8EYD?tab=summary&timestamp=752.0)
      - [**Process:** Aida will export image samples to Figma for review.](https://fathom.video/share/nadTYn2QH2smPEMFxjzDh2RpgXdA8EYD?tab=summary&timestamp=928.0)
  - [**Contentful Uploads:**](https://fathom.video/share/nadTYn2QH2smPEMFxjzDh2RpgXdA8EYD?tab=summary&timestamp=968.0)
      - [**Requirement:** GrowthX must add author initials (e.g., RH) to drafts in Contentful.](https://fathom.video/share/nadTYn2QH2smPEMFxjzDh2RpgXdA8EYD?tab=summary&timestamp=968.0)
      - [**Rationale:** An empty author field blocks other users from publishing content.](https://fathom.video/share/nadTYn2QH2smPEMFxjzDh2RpgXdA8EYD?tab=summary&timestamp=968.0)
  - [**Source Control:**](https://fathom.video/share/nadTYn2QH2smPEMFxjzDh2RpgXdA8EYD?tab=summary&timestamp=1814.0)
      - [**Issue:** Hex requested control over source quality, citing past issues with another agency using unknown publications.](https://fathom.video/share/nadTYn2QH2smPEMFxjzDh2RpgXdA8EYD?tab=summary&timestamp=1814.0)
      - [**Solution:** GrowthX can configure Atlas to pull from a client-approved list of primary sources (e.g., McKinsey, Gartner).](https://fathom.video/share/nadTYn2QH2smPEMFxjzDh2RpgXdA8EYD?tab=summary&timestamp=1814.0)

## Next Steps

  - [**Jessica:**](https://fathom.video/share/nadTYn2QH2smPEMFxjzDh2RpgXdA8EYD?tab=summary&timestamp=682.0)
      - [Confirm Rachel as the article author.](https://fathom.video/share/nadTYn2QH2smPEMFxjzDh2RpgXdA8EYD?tab=summary&timestamp=682.0)
      - [Provide the list of preferred primary sources.](https://fathom.video/share/nadTYn2QH2smPEMFxjzDh2RpgXdA8EYD?tab=summary&timestamp=1814.0)
  - [**Peter:**](https://fathom.video/share/nadTYn2QH2smPEMFxjzDh2RpgXdA8EYD?tab=summary&timestamp=752.0)
      - [Get brand approval from Annie for featured images.](https://fathom.video/share/nadTYn2QH2smPEMFxjzDh2RpgXdA8EYD?tab=summary&timestamp=752.0)
  - [**Aida:**](https://fathom.video/share/nadTYn2QH2smPEMFxjzDh2RpgXdA8EYD?tab=summary&timestamp=928.0)
      - [Export image samples to Figma for review.](https://fathom.video/share/nadTYn2QH2smPEMFxjzDh2RpgXdA8EYD?tab=summary&timestamp=928.0)
      - [Create a Notion doc with the content roadmap and prioritization logic.](https://fathom.video/share/nadTYn2QH2smPEMFxjzDh2RpgXdA8EYD?tab=summary&timestamp=1046.0)
  - [**GrowthX:**](https://fathom.video/share/nadTYn2QH2smPEMFxjzDh2RpgXdA8EYD?tab=summary&timestamp=968.0)
      - [Add author initials to all Contentful drafts.](https://fathom.video/share/nadTYn2QH2smPEMFxjzDh2RpgXdA8EYD?tab=summary&timestamp=968.0)
